title: Machine learning potential as a guide for eutectic in ultra-refractory multicomponent ceramics
authors: V. E. Valiulin, A. V. Mikheyenkov, N. M. Chtchelkatchev, E. A. Levashov
abstract: The experimental determination of eutectic points is a long-established and widely used technique, but it is generally only practical for systems with relatively low melting points. Many modern, promising materials, however, are ultra-refractory, with melting points exceeding 3000 K. For these systems, conventional melting experiments become prohibitively expensive and technically challenging. Advanced AI modeling can serve as a powerful precursor to guide successful experimentation in such cases. This work proposes a novel criterion for determining the eutectic point concentration in ultra-refractory alloys. The approach is verified using the Ti-B-C system - the most thoroughly studied three-component refractory system to date. The core of the algorithm is a machine-learning interatomic potential, based on a neural network, which achieves accuracy comparable to ab initio methods. Crucially, the algorithm operates effectively in the liquid phase, eliminating the need for information about the solid alloy's crystalline structure to estimate eutectic points.
summary: This work proposes using machine learning potentials to overcome the experimental difficulty of finding eutectic points in ultra-refractory alloys. By simulating the supercooled liquid state, the authors identified characteristic signatures in the concentration derivatives of thermodynamic and transport properties. This provides a powerful, computationally guided tool for materials discovery in extreme conditions.
main_problem: The primary challenge is determining eutectic points in ultra-refractory multicomponent ceramics, as conventional melting experiments are too costly and technically difficult for materials with melting points exceeding 3000 K.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated that a machine-learning interatomic potential (MLIP) can accurately predict eutectic compositions by analyzing liquid-phase thermodynamic and transport properties.
method: The approach uses MLIPs, trained on DFT data, to simulate the supercooled liquid state and identify eutectic regions by analyzing concentration derivatives of properties like density and energy.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the Ti-B-C system, analyzing pseudo-binary sections ($	ext{TiB}_2-	ext{TiC}$ and $	ext{TiB}_2-	ext{C}$). The core computational tool is a neural network-based MLIP, validated against high-accuracy DFT methods.
key_observables: Physical density, total energy, diffusion coefficient, bulk modulus, and viscosity coefficient, analyzed via their concentration derivatives.
important_parameters: Ultra-refractory temperature regimes (above 3000 K); the supercooled liquid state ($T=2500$ K) is used for simulation.
main_assumptions: The method assumes that local extrema in the concentration derivatives of liquid properties reliably pinpoint the eutectic composition, bypassing the need for solid-state structural information.
figures_summary: Figures show pseudo-binary sections and liquid phase diagrams, MLIP performance metrics (RMSE), and plots illustrating the concentration dependence of various properties (density, energy, etc.) highlighting the eutectic region.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem, details the MLIP methodology (training, validation against other ML potentials), applies the method to the Ti-B-C system, and concludes by showing that characteristic anomalies in liquid properties confirm the eutectic location.
why_interesting: While focused on condensed matter thermodynamics, the core methodology—using deep learning potentials to model complex, high-temperature, multi-component liquid states—is highly relevant to simulating non-equilibrium dynamics in quantum many-body systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Validation of the machine-learning interatomic po- tential (MLIP) accuracy. The figure reports the model’s performance on the combined training and test datasets. When evaluated strictly on the held-out test configurations, the model achieves a root-mean-square error (RMSE) of 14.2 meV/atom for energy and 0.5 eV/˚A for forces. The re- ported force error is relatively high, which can be attributed to the complexity of simulating a high-temperature liquid alloy composed of chemically distinct elements (Ti, alongside the inherently incompressible elements boron and carbon), com- bined with the use of a limited training dataset. Nevertheless, the potential successfully passes a series...

FIG. 3. Accuracy assessment of the DeepMD-se-a2 MLIP via radial distribution function (RDF) benchmark. Total and partial radial distribution functions for a liquid Ti40B40C20 alloy (obtained as a 1:1 molar mixture of TiB2 and TiC) at T = 4000 K. The computationally efficient descriptor-based DeepMD-se-a2 potential is compared against two high-accuracy graph-based foundation models, DeepMD-DPA3 (DPA-3.1-3M) and MACE (mace-mp-0b3-medium), both fine-tuned on the DeepMD-se-a2 training dataset. The close agreement between all calculated RDFs confirms the accuracy of the optimized DeepMD-se-a2 MLIP, despite its relatively high reported force error of 0.5 eV/˚A. Additional benchmarks are presented...

FIG. 4. Accuracy assessment of the DeepMD-se-a2 MLIP via velocity autocorrelation function (VACF) benchmark. The computationally efficient descriptor-based DeepMD-se-a2 potential is compared against two different high-accuracy graph- based MLIP foundation models DeepMD-DPA3 (DPA-3.1-3M) and MACE (mace-mp-0b3-medium), both fine-tuned on the DeepMD-se-a2 training-test dataset. (a) VACF for a liquid Ti74B16C10 alloy at T = 4000 K, computed in the NVT ensemble. The inset shows the Fourier transform of the VACF; the low-frequency noise is attributed to the finite simulation trajectory length (10 ps). For panels (b)–(d), computations were performed in the NPT ensemble. The larger discrepancy...
